notes.anchor: the Senate in full session was inspired in denying the thanksgiving to Gabinius
notes.note: A. Gabinius, consul 58 BC and governor of Syria, requested a supplicatio (public thanksgiving) for his campaigns; the Senate's refusal was a stinging rebuff to one of Cicero's bitterest enemies, who as consul had abetted his exile.

notes.anchor: Procilius swears that this has happened to no man before
notes.note: Procilius, a Roman senator, attests that no commander had ever before been refused a thanksgiving --- underscoring how exceptional the Senate's snub of Gabinius was.

notes.anchor: the Campanian-land affair would be brought on
notes.note: The recurring fight over the public land of Campania, distributed under Caesar's law of 59 BC; reopening it threatened the triumvirs' settlement, so its repeated postponement was politically charged.

notes.anchor: In this case my water sticks
notes.note: A courtroom metaphor: a speaker's time was measured by a water-clock (clepsydra), so 'my water sticks' means he is stuck, unsure how to proceed --- here on the Campanian-land question.
